1.synthesis: Paul takes up the matter of credentials: "Are we beginning to commend ourselves again? Or do we need… letters of recommendation?" (v.1). His credential is the Corinthians themselves: "You are our letter… written on our hearts," indeed "a letter from Christ… written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (vv.2–3). Such confidence is "through Christ toward God" — "not that we are sufficient in ourselves… but our sufficiency is from God, who has made us sufficient to be ministers of a new covenant, not of the letter but of the Spirit. For the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life" (vv.4–6). The transformed church is living proof of a Spirit-empowered ministry.
1.historical_context: Letters of recommendation (v.1) were a standard institution in the ancient world — a prominent person vouching in writing for a traveling associate. Paul's rivals carried such letters; Paul, lacking that social network, points instead to the changed lives of the Corinthians (v.2). The contrast of "tablets of stone" with "tablets of human hearts" (v.3) directly evokes the new-covenant promises of a law written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33) and a heart of flesh for a heart of stone (Ezekiel 36:26). "Sufficiency" (hikanotēs, v.5) was the term for a tradesman's qualification, and "new covenant" (v.6) echoes both Jeremiah and the Last Supper (1 Corinthians 11:25).
1.literary_structure: This answers the "Who is sufficient?" of 2 Corinthians 2:16 — sufficiency is "from God" (v.5) — and pivots the ministry-defense onto the theme of the new covenant that dominates the chapter. The "letter" wordplay is deft: from "letters of recommendation" (v.1) to the Corinthians as a living "letter" of Christ (v.3) to the deadly "letter" of the law versus the life-giving Spirit (v.6). The stone/heart and letter/Spirit antitheses set up the extended Moses comparison of vv.7–18.
1.theological_application: The truest credential of a ministry is transformed lives, not impressive paperwork: a church changed by the Spirit is "a letter from Christ" the world can read (v.3). Paul's confession that "our sufficiency is from God" (v.5) frees the servant from the burden of self-reliance and the temptation of self-commendation — all fruitfulness is God's gift. And the contrast "the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life" (v.6) warns that even God's holy law, apart from the Spirit, can only expose and condemn sin; new life comes only by the Spirit of the new covenant.
1.christology: The living letter is authored by Christ and merely delivered by Paul (v.3): the Spirit who raised Jesus writes resurrection life on human hearts, fulfilling the new-covenant promise of Ezekiel 36:26–27. The "new covenant" Paul serves was inaugurated in Christ's blood at the Last Supper (1 Corinthians 11:25), so his ministry mediates the very life Christ secured. Sufficiency "through Christ toward God" (v.4) means apart from him the apostle can do nothing (cf. John 15:5); every fruit is a participation in Christ's own life by the Spirit.

7.synthesis: Paul compares the two covenants by their glory. If "the ministry of death, carved in letters on stone, came with such glory" that Israel could not gaze at Moses' shining face — "a glory now being brought to an end" — "will not the ministry of the Spirit have even more glory?" (vv.7–8). The surpassing glory of the new covenant gives "great boldness" (v.12), unlike Moses, who veiled his fading glory (v.13). To this day a veil lies over the hearts of those who read the old covenant, "but when one turns to the Lord, the veil is removed" (vv.14–16). "Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom" (v.17); and we all, "beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another" (v.18).
7.historical_context: Paul builds on the account of Moses' shining face after meeting God on Sinai, which so alarmed Israel that he veiled it (Exodus 34:29–35). His argument is a rabbinic qal vahomer ("how much more," v.8): if the lesser, fading Mosaic glory was glorious, the greater, permanent glory of the Spirit's ministry far surpasses it. This is salvation-historical ordering, not anti-Torah polemic — Paul cites Torah approvingly throughout. He reads the veil (vv.13–14) as concealing the fading of the old glory, and the "hardening" of minds (v.14) echoes prophetic language (Isaiah 6:9–10). "Turns to the Lord" (v.16) uses the standard verb for conversion (cf. Exodus 34:34, where Moses removed the veil before the LORD).
7.literary_structure: The section is a sustained, contrastive midrash on Exodus 34, structured by the repeated motif of glory and the symbol of the veil. The argument moves from the relative glory of the two ministries (vv.7–11) to the boldness it produces (v.12), to the veil over Israel's reading (vv.13–15), to its removal in conversion (vv.16–17), climaxing in the transformation of believers (v.18). The much-debated identification "the Lord is the Spirit" (v.17) closes the gap between the "Lord" Israel turned to and the Spirit who frees, and the climactic "being transformed" sets up the treasure-in-clay paradox of chapter 4.
7.theological_application: The Christian stands in a ministry of surpassing and permanent glory (v.11), and so may serve with "great boldness" (v.12) rather than the veiled caution of the old order. The veil over unbelieving hearts is removed only by turning to the Lord (v.16), so Scripture itself remains opaque apart from Christ. And the goal of the new covenant is transformation: "beholding the glory of the Lord," believers are progressively changed into his image "from one degree of glory to another" (v.18) — sanctification is the Spirit's work of conforming us to Christ as we gaze on him.
7.christology: Christ is the hermeneutical key to the whole Old Testament: only when one turns to him is the veil lifted (vv.14–16), for the risen Lord who "opened the Scriptures" on the Emmaus road (Luke 24:27) enables their right reading. The glory believers behold and are changed into is the glory of the Lord — and the image into which they are transformed is Christ himself (Romans 8:29). Where Moses came away with a fading, reflected radiance, in Christ every believer beholds God's glory directly (John 1:14) and is remade in his likeness by the Spirit of the Lord (v.18).
